In Volume I of Wojo’s Weapons, we dedicated the first part of the book to
examining the Closed Catalan. The reasons for this were twofold: first, the Closed
Catalan is the opening readers are most likely to face at the club level after 1.43
d5; and second, the Closed Catalan was also the most thematic of the openings
we presented, meaning that it could be readily be broken down into separate
themes. In Volume II, we seek to do the same thing with the most common club-
level responses to the King’s Indian — those that feature the move ...Ab8-d7 early
on from Black.

This chapter focuses on what happens when Black attempts to counter White’s
dynamic Fianchetto King’s Indian setup with ...Ab8-d7, ...e7-e5, and ...exd4
(without following up with ...a7-a6, as in the Gallagher Variation, which we cover
in Chapter 3). Those readers who have played over the games presented in David
Bronstein’s classic Zurich 1953 tournament book will undoubtedly remember
the stubborn, resistant position that Black tries to organize along the last three
ranks in this variation. The following oft-quoted passage has served for decades
as the definitive description of Black’s strategy here:

Characteristically, the King’s Indian Defense features a tense battle waged
on all fronts simultaneously. The system used here secures White considerable
territory, not only in the center, but on the kingside as well.

I do not wish to leave the reader the false impression that White’s further
task, which is to transform his sizeable spatial plus into a material advantage,
will be an easy one. The secret of the King’s Indian’s hardihood is that, while

12
15:
THE “ZURICH 1953” DEFENSE

conceding space, Black builds a few small but weighty details into his configura-
tion. Foremost among these are his long-range bishops at g7 and c8, his firmly
entrenched knight at c5 and the rook at e8, which maintain constant watch on
the e-pawn. Nor ought we to forget his pawns. The “weak” pawn on d6 is just
waiting for the chance to push to d5, so White must continually keep an eye on
that... [The black a-pawn] also has an important role: the threat to advance it...
can upset his opponent’s plans for that sector at any time, so White must take
extra precautions regarding the defense of c3 and c4.

Much has changed since the 1950s, however. This chapter is devoted entirely
to demonstrating how Wojo did indeed convert his “sizeable spatial plus” into
something more concrete, either in the form of material or an attack. Let’s begin
by examining the most obvious way that White can turn his space into material:

winning the d6-pawn.

Black’s Pawn on d6 Falls

There are two common ways by which
Black’s d6-pawn, during the course of
the middlegame, may fall into White’s
hands. The first is that Black simply
overlooks something and ends up
hanging it. This is surprisingly com-
mon even at the master level. Defend-
ing the d6é-pawn becomes a challenge
for Black at times, and if he makes
some slight inaccuracies, the d-pawn
dies a miserable death. Consider the
following position, taken from Wojt-
kiewicz-Souleidis, Duisburg 1999:

xi i 8
ZG A

Although Souleidis would go on to
earn the title of International Master

that same year, defending the d6-pawn
proved too difficult for him from this
cramped position. He went astray
with 22...4f8?!, which, among other
things, takes the f8 square away from
Black’s bishop on g7. After 23.Wd2
&d7 24.4f3, Black found himself
unable to stop White from crashing
through on d6. He soon resigned after
24...8ad8 25.Wxd6 Wc8 26.2b6
Sxh3 27.2xd8 Wg4 28.2xf6 Yxg3
29.8d2 &2xf6 30.e5 1-0.

The second way that the d6-pawn
can fall is that, as Black is slowly
squeezed against the edge of the board
by White’s impending space advantage,
he finds himself incapable of defending
it. We illustrate this in the third game
in this chapter, Wojtkiewicz-Shulman,
after showing two games featuring
tactical themes allowing White to crack
the d6-pawn early on. Finally, the last
game presented in this section shows
Black’s d-pawn falling for a combina-
tion of the two reasons.

9...We7

This is too passive. The more ambi-
tious 9...%b6 is Black’s best attempt,
after which White can choose from
among 10.8e1 (Wojo’s usual choice),
10.d5, and the crazy 10.c5. In Chapter
5, which goes over various move or-
ders, we also examine one of Dean’s
favorite lines here, 10.2b1!?.

10.2e3
It is worth noting at this point that

there exists a huge misconception
among club players that White’s dark-

14

squared bishop belongs on the long di-
agonal and that he should fianchetto it
with b2-b3 and 2c1-b2. Although such
a plan is still occasionally employed at
grandmaster level, it is not White’s best
idea in this position. Black is aiming
to play ...exd4 and open the center, so
White should be seeking to neutralize
Black’s control of the a1-h8 diagonal by
stepping off it — not by adding pieces
to it for Black’s g7-bishop to attack.
From the position in the previous dia-
gram, the e3 square is almost always
the best one for White’s own dark-
squared bishop. From there, it eyes the
queenside (particularly the vulnerable
b6 square) and discourages skirmishes
such as ...%d8-b6-b4. Furthermore,
should Black become ambitious and try
...7-h5-h4 to take a potshot at White’s
kingside, it would help to be able to
post the clergyman to the g5 square.
Wojo sometimes developed the bishop
to {4 in order to provoke Black into
wasting time (such as with ...Af6-h5),
but ultimately the bishop was usually
relocated to e3.

It is hard to say exactly why so
many players choose to fianchetto the
dark-squared bishop as White in these
structures, but perhaps it has to do with
a psychological defect of some sort.
White players take comfort in devel-
oping both bishops to their respective
long diagonals, and feel they are “coun-
tering” Black’s dark-squared bishop on
g7 by placing their own on b2. One look
at Bronstein and Neat’s 1999 work Play
the King’s Indian ought to cure any
player of White of this attitude. The
authors pick games featuring White’s
dark-squared bishop developing to b2
in order to demonstrate the potential of
Black’s position — for instance, in one

...4a8x(2)al exchange sacrifice:

26.0xe8) 24.8a2 Wxg3+ 25.eh1 Yxc3
26.8a3 2xh3 27.Bxb3 2xg2+ 28.hoxg2

Of course, this doesn’t mean that
White should never develop his dark-
squared bishop to b2 in the Fianchetto
King’s Indian. There are plenty of
situations where b3 and 2b2 are ap-
propriate. In general, however, they
occur only in variations such as the
Panno (with an early ...Ac6, ...a6, and
..4b8 from Black), when the second
player isn’t particularly aiming for a
quick ...e7-e5xd4. And with that, let’s
return to the game.

19:
THE “ZURICH 1953” DEFENSE

227 5.2g2 0-0 6.0-0 Abd7 7.Ac3
e5 8.e4 c6 9.2b1

A“Wojo Weapon” that allows White
to get the kind of positional game that
he is looking for, without stepping into
a sea of complications. Placing the rook
on bl takes the sting out of Black’s
tactical shots early on: his rook is off
the al-h8 diagonal, and his b-pawn is
defended against any ...%d8-b6 sortie.
Meanwhile, the rook is useful on bl
because it helps prepare the gradual
b2-b3-b4 push later in the middle-
game. The disadvantage of this move
is that it may not be useful for White if
Black doesn’t concede the center with
9...exd4 and instead goes about prepar-
ing the ...b7-b5 punch with something
like 9...a6. We look at this type of ap-
proach for Black in Chapter 4.

9...exd4 10.Axd4 8e8 11.h3 Ac5

Black can try to take advantage of
White’s “slow” move 9 with 11...ab6!?
12.b3 d5, a break in the center which
has been tried a handful of times. After
13.exd5 cxd5 14.c5, White retains his
edge: 14...abd7 15.b4 a5 16.a3 gave
White the upper hand in F.Sage-C.
Vasile, corr. 2004, while 14...de4!?
15.Axe4 dxe4 16.4b5 gives White a
large initiative.

12.8e1 a5 13.b3

This patient approach is the best
one. White prevents any attempt at
...a9-a4 from Black: he can now sim-
ply push past with b3-b4, dislodging
Black’s knight from c5. White is also
slowly removing his pieces from the
al-h8 diagonal.

13...Afd7

Black prepares for some maneu-
vering. 13...h6, a waiting move, has also
been tried. White usually piles up on
the d6-pawn with 14.2f4, after which
he has a comfortable game.

14.2e3 Df6?!

Now it becomes apparent that
Black is just going back and forth.
More common is 14...4e5 15.4c2 We7
16.2bd1 “ed7 17.f4 and so on, when
again it seems like Black isn’t making
any progress.

15.W%c2 We7 16.kbd1 2d7 17.2f4

White is fully developed, so he de-
cides to turn the screws on d6. Black,
who has lost two moves by playing
...@f6-d7 and back again, will be hard-
pressed to find an adequate defense.

31...2f8 32.We3 Wxe3 33.fxe3
b5 34.2d5 2b8 35.28a6 &2c3 36.2ad6
©f7 37.2d8 2b6 38.25d6 1-0

After White forces the trade of
rooks, it’s all over.

White’s prophylactic 9.8b1 avoids
many of the tactical ideas players of
the black pieces like to employ. Even
though the rook had to move again
with 16.8bd1 in order to come to the
center, this slight loss of time didn’t
mean much compared to the time
Black lost with the ill-fated ... “f6-d7-
f6 shuffle. In this game, White slowly
gained the upper hand just by putting
his pieces on good squares and eyeing
the d6-pawn. Once again, the tactical
shot @d4-b5 arose, ruining Black’s
position.
21:
THE “ZURICH 1953” DEFENSE

In the next game, Wojo must slowly
force his grandmaster opponent back
by taking space on both sides of the
board before he is allowed to harvest
the d6-pawn. This game was first an-
notated in Jonathan Hilton’s original
“How Wojo Won’ series for Chess Life
Online (see Part III, published April
2007), and it reappears here with the
notes significantly revised.

Wojtkiewicz, Aleksander (2530)
Shulman, Yuri (2490)

[E69] Katowice 1995

1.Af3 Df6 2.c4 g6 3.g3 227

7.0Ac3 e5 8.e4 c6 9.2b1 a5 10.h3

We7 14.8e2

nein

A useful waiting move, preparing
to put pressure on the d-pawn with an
eventual 2c1-f4 and #e2-d2. Note that
Black was lining up along the e-file.
With 14.8e2, White places his rook on
a square where it is defended by his two
knights, preventing any tactical ideas
from Black involving ...d6-d5. 14.2f4
first is somewhat more common, when
only after 14...afd7 is 15.2e2 played.
Then 15...Ae5 16.2e3! allows White to

hit Black’s knight back with f2-f4 fol-
lowed by &e3-f2.

14...2d7 15.2f4 Dh5 16.2e3

White has succeeded in luring
Black’s knight out to the awkward h5
square.

16...2ad8 17.Wd2 Wf8 18.2d1
£c8 19.%e2

Overprotecting the e4-pawn will be
important in the struggle for control of
the kingside.

19...h6 20.2h2 Af6 21.21 Ah5S
22 feel Yh7 23.a3

Black has maneuvered all of his
pieces to the kingside, so Wojo turns
his attention to the queenside. The
absence of Black’s knight on h5 from
the activity in the center, along with
his rook being on d8 (rather than on
a8) and his queen on f8 (rather than
c7) will make it hard for him to defend
against White’s impending b3-b4-b5
expansion.

23...4h8

Black begins an interesting attempt
to generate counterplay. Anticipat-
ing White’s idea of advancing to b4
and b5, Black exerts pressure on the
queenside dark squares, which White
must leave behind in order to carry on
with his plans.

24.b4 axb4 25.axb4 Dab 26.b5

White begins to break down Black’s
position by undermining the d5 square.

26...Ab4 27.4b3 c5 28.ade2

28...f5

Black has succeeded in plugging up
the queenside, stymying White’s attack
there. Now, he seeks counterplay on
the kingside to relieve his cramped
position. His d6-pawn remains weak,
however. As White continues to push
for more space, Black will find it harder
and harder to hold his backward d-
pawn. Wojo begins by doubling on
the d-file.

29,.8d2 Af6 30.8ed1 WS 31.exf5
&xf5 32.¢4

White continues to gain space.
Having forced Black to defend d6,
White switches his attention to slowly
advancing on the kingside. One of his
main ideas is the knight maneuver
e2-f4, further controlling d5.

32...2e4 33.0f4 &xg2 34.kxg2

Black’s position has seemed more
or less defensible up to this point, but
now Black struggles with the pressure
on his position. How can he defend
both his g6-pawn (against White’s
upcoming “b3-b1) and his d6-pawn
(against White’s powerful doubled
rooks)? Unfortunately, at this point
there is no good answer. For instance,

on f5 and h5.

35.Dfe2 We7 36.4g3 Wed 37.0f5

White is getting closer and closer to

37...2f8 38.f3

White methodically prepares to put
his bishop on the h2-b8 diagonal.

38...b6 39.221 We6 40.8h2 &g8

There was nothing better. The d6-
pawn could not be saved.

Following the collapse of the d-

42...2xd6 43.8xd6 Of4+ 44.Hf1
23:
THE “ZURICH 1953” DEFENSE

Rxd6 45.8xd6 We5 46.Wd1 Deb6
47.8d7 &h8 48.Axh6 Wh2 49.4f7+
&g7 50.0fxg5+ &g6 51.0xe6
Wxh3+ 52.eg1 8xe6 53.Wb1 Wh6
54.4d6+ 1-0

White’s cautious play in the early
middlegame paid off. Although some
of his maneuvers shuffling back and
forth may have seemed a little eccen-
tric, his 23.a3 and 24.b4 ultimately
caught Black off-guard. With space-
taking moves like 26.b5 and 32.94,
White was able to push Black back.
Eventually, Black had too little ma-
neuvering room to adequately defend
his backward d-pawn. As is par for
the course, the fall of this pawn meant
Black’s rapid demise.

Depending on the amount of resis-
tance Black puts up, White may not
have to wait all the way to move 41 to
see the black d-pawn fall. When Black
is an amateur rather than a grand-
master, his position can deteriorate
far more quickly. In the last game of
this section, we admire the speed with
which Black’s fortress crumbles in the
hands of a non-master.

Wojtkiewicz, Aleksander (2540)
Werner, Lennart

[E69] Stockholm 1991

29.4d4 1-0

In Wojtkiewicz-Werner, White had no trouble gaining the advantage. Black

found himself forced to play ...2a8-d8 and ...2d7-c8 in order to defend the d6-
pawn, a maneuver which left his queenside vulnerable. After 23.0a4, Black’s
position collapsed.

Conclusion: In all four of the above games, something went dreadfully
wrong for Black. Either he made an inaccuracy or two that allowed White to
crash through on d6, or he was slowly pushed off the board and forced to cede
the d-pawn, or both. With patience and a keen eye for penetrating weaknesses
in Black’s position, Wojo was able to convert his space advantage into something

more tangible.

White Breaks Through on the Kingside

When Black successfully finds a way to
defend his d6-pawn, White must cre-
ate — as the old saying goes — a “second
weakness.” Often, weaknesses on the
queenside — such as the a5-pawn or the
b6 square — prove sufficient for White
to penetrate Black’s position. But when
Black proves solid, White often begins
expanding on the kingside (much as
in Wojtkiewicz-Shulman above, only
earlier in the middlegame) as a way to

22

increase his influence over the board.

Wojo’s formula for victory using
such an approach was straightforward:
first, tie Black to the defense of the d6-
pawn; then, advance the pawns on the
kingside. In order to accomplish the
first goal, he would bring his queen’s
rook to di and then, after shoring up
his e4-pawn by way of &fl-el, f2-f4,
and &e3-f2, gently slide his queen
overfromc2 to d2. From there, all that
25:
THE “ZURICH 1953” DEFENSE

remained was to remove the knight
from d4 in order to unleash White’s
buildup along the d-file. In order to
defend, Black would either have to
play ...2c8-d7, ...2a8-d8, and ...2d7-
c8 again (which, as we know from
Wojtkiewicz-Werner above, weakens
the a5-pawn) or tie himself down by
putting his active king’s bishop on ei-
ther f8 or e7. If Black chose the latter,
Wojo had the “all clear” to begin an
orderly assault on the kingside. Let’s
begin with a typical example.

This game is a typical example
of how Wojo would use an effective
kingside pawn storm to wear Black
down in this line. He began by tying
Black down to the d6-pawn, but after
that, he expanded with g3-g4, ®e2-g3,
292-f3, &g1-g2, and h3-h4-h5. Black
cracked under the pressure and went
down a pawn. Then, in the end, White
broke through to Black’s king in any
case, eventually securing mate with
59. Bxf8+.

The following game reiterates these
themes. Black’s defense is somewhat
less stubborn and White’s attack comes
more quickly, however.

In this game, White’s well organized forces tied Black down to the defense
of the d-pawn early on. The standard maneuver f2-f4, 2e3-f2, ®d4-e2, and
Wc2-d2 was executed by White without any serious opposition from Black.
White was able to convert his pressure (by way of moves such as 23.@xe6 and
25.f5) into a promising kingside attack, which he botched by backing down
from 32.95. Inthe endgame, Black had some chances to defend, but ultimately
White’s continued kingside pressure (and Black’s lack of queenside develo pment)
allowed White to break through to the last three ranks.

27
30:
CHAPTER 1

Conclusion: When Black is chained to the defense of his d-pawn, White often
has the possibility of expanding on the kingside. After completing development,
players of White should be looking for kingside pawn storm ideas. Ultimately,
however, White should remember that his power comes from his control of the
center and his extra space on both sides of the board. A timely a2-a3 and b3-
b4 thrust can do just as much to disrupt Black’s defenses as running down the

white h-pawn.

A Classic Revisited

Despite the apparent simplicity of
White’s formula for tying Black down
to d6, by the time Wojo actually em-
ployed it beginning in the 1980s and
90s, it was already several decades in
the making. It is worth briefly revisiting
the classic game Stahlberg-Boleslavsky
from the Zurich Candidates’ Tourna-
ment of 1953, to see how much players
of White have learned since the old
days. The following game — which was
phenomenally annotated by Bronstein
in the Zurich 1953 tournament book
(perhaps the first great work written
“about” the King’s Indian Defense)
— had a profound impact on the en-
tire line with ...Ab8-d7, ...e7-e5, and
...e5xd4, so much so that we have
dubbed Black’s setup here the “Zurich
1953 Defense” for the purposes of this
book. In it, we see White struggling
with uncomfortable pressure on the
queenside.

Stahlberg, Gideon
Boleslavsky, Isaak

[E69] Candidates’ Tournament,
Zurich 1953

1.d4 Of6 2.c4 g6 3.23 827 4.222
0-0 5.4c3 d6 6.4f3 Abd7 7.0-0 e5
8.e4 Ze8 9.h3 exd4 10.4xd4 4c5
11.8e1 a5

28

12.%c2

It is worth mentioning that this
does not hang the e4-pawn to 12...
“fxe4, as 13.Axe4 &xd4 14.825 Wd7
(not 14...f6? 15.2xf6) 15.Af6+ &xf6
16.2xf6 He6 17.2c3 is known to give
White excellent compensation on the
dark squares.

Although the text move remained
the main line for many years, it has
the drawback of allowing Black to play
the space-gaining ...a5-a4. Wojo would
simply play the more modern 12.2b1
instead, when 12...a4 could be met by
13.Adb5 &d7 14.2f4 with pressure.
For instance, T.Markowski-A.Mista,
Warsaw2004, went 14...2xb5 15.cxb5!
&fd7 16.¥4c2, when White had the two
bishops, more space, and a slightly
more active game.
